The Invoice Pipeline

When a supplier invoice file is uploaded or picked up from the watch folder, InvoiceProcessingService::process(Document $doc) runs it through seven sequential steps. This page describes each step in detail. Understanding the pipeline is useful for troubleshooting extraction problems, tuning confidence thresholds, and writing posting rules.

Pipeline overview

  1. Text extraction — read the file's content into a plain text string
  2. File-type detection — verify the file type using Magika; reject unsupported formats
  3. LLM extraction — send the text to Claude; receive structured line items, amounts, and a confidence score
  4. Supplier resolution — match the invoice to an existing supplier or create a pending one
  5. Account resolution — assign a GL account to each line item
  6. Exchange rates — apply a rate if the invoice is in a foreign currency
  7. Posting rule evaluation — check whether the invoice qualifies for automatic posting

Text extraction

The DocumentTextExtractor service reads the invoice file and returns its text content as a plain string. It uses different methods depending on the file format:

  • PDF — text is extracted by PdfExtractor, which reads the embedded text layer
  • DOCX, XLSX, CSV — parsed by the paperdoc-dev/paperdoc-lib package

The extracted text is what gets sent to the LLM in step 3.

File-type detection (Magika)

MagikaService shells out to the Magika CLI binary to detect the actual file type from the file content (not just the file extension). This guards against misnamed files — for example, an HTML file renamed to .pdf. If the detected file type is not in Merlin's supported list, an InvalidFileTypeException is thrown and the invoice is marked as failed before any extraction attempt.

Magika must be installed as a system binary. Its location is configured via the MAGIKA_BINARY environment variable (default: magika, meaning it must be on the system path).

LLM extraction

LlmService sends the extracted text to the Claude API using the prompt template at resources/views/prompts/invoice-extraction.blade.php. Claude returns a structured ExtractedInvoice data object containing: supplier details, invoice date, due date, currency, a list of line items (each with description, quantity, unit price, tax rate, and line total), totals, and a confidence score between 0 and 1. Each call is logged to an LlmLog row, including the model used, token counts, duration, and the confidence score.

Tiered model fallback

Every extraction attempt starts with the fast model (ANTHROPIC_MODEL_FAST, configured in .env — typically Claude Haiku). If the fast model's response is unsatisfactory, the extraction is retried from scratch using the strong model (ANTHROPIC_MODEL — typically Claude Sonnet). A fast-model response is considered unsatisfactory if any of the following are true:

  • The response is not valid JSON
  • The extracted line totals do not reconcile to the invoice's reported total (see Reconciliation below)
  • The confidence score is below the fallback_confidence threshold (default 0.80, configurable in Settings › Purchasing)

If the fast model produces a reconciling result, it is kept even if its confidence is lower than the strong model's result. A reconciling fast result is always preferred over a non-reconciling strong result.

Reconciliation

After extraction, Merlin checks whether the sum of the extracted line totals (each grossed up by its effective tax rate) matches the invoice's reported total. Reconciliation is checked against the invoice total only — not against the subtotal or the tax total separately. This is deliberate: VAT-inclusive invoices and invoices where shipping is shown outside the subtotal both break subtotal-based arithmetic, but the grand total is always authoritative.

If the sum of lines equals the total (within a small floating-point tolerance) the extraction is considered reconciling. If it does not, the fast model's result is discarded and the strong model is tried.

Model health and retirement

Separate from the quality fallback, ModelHealthService handles the case where a model ID has been retired by Anthropic. When a call returns a 404 not_found_error, the failing model is circuit-broken (marked unavailable in cache for ANTHROPIC_MODEL_DOWN_TTL seconds), an alert email is sent to ANTHROPIC_ALERT_RECIPIENTS, and the call escalates to the next rung of the ladder: Haiku → Sonnet → Opus (ANTHROPIC_MODEL_FAST → ANTHROPIC_MODEL → ANTHROPIC_MODEL_BACKUP). The models:health-check Artisan command probes all three models daily at 05:30. See Configuration — Model health and fallback for how to fix a retired model ID.

Supplier resolution

SupplierResolver tries to match the supplier details extracted from the invoice to an existing supplier in the database. The resolution order is:

  1. Tax number match — exact match on the supplier's tax/VAT registration number
  2. Name similarity — fuzzy text matching against existing supplier names
  3. Create pending supplier — if no match is found, PartyService creates a new supplier record with status = pending using the name extracted from the invoice

Pending suppliers must be approved by a staff member before the invoice can be fully processed. See Suppliers — Pending suppliers.

Account resolution

AccountResolver assigns a GL account to each extracted line item. The resolution order is:

  1. History match — if this supplier has previously posted a line with a similar description to the same account, that account is used
  2. LLM suggestion — the line description is sent to Claude to suggest an account from the chart of accounts
  3. Manual allocation fallback — if neither history nor the LLM can suggest an account, the line is left unallocated and must be assigned manually before the invoice can be posted

Exchange rates

ExchangeRateService fetches the exchange rate from ExchangeRate-API when an invoice is in a foreign currency (i.e. a currency other than the base currency set in Settings › General). Rates are cached for 24 hours to avoid repeated API calls. The EXCHANGERATE_API_KEY environment variable must be set for this step to work.

Posting rule evaluation

PostingRuleService evaluates all active posting rules against the invoice after line items and accounts have been resolved. If a matching rule's actions include auto_post, and the invoice's confidence score meets the autopost_confidence threshold (default 0.90, configurable in Settings › Purchasing), the invoice is approved and posted automatically without human intervention. See Posting Rules for how to configure rules.

VAT handling

All amounts are stored excluding VAT in the database. The printed invoice amount is authoritative — Merlin never re-derives totals from tax rates applied to stored values, because floating-point rounding would cause cent-level discrepancies.

The LLM prompt instructs Claude to extract amounts as shown on the invoice, not pre-stripped of VAT. After extraction, Merlin detects whether the invoice lines are VAT-inclusive (i.e. whether the sum of line totals approximately equals the invoice total and a tax total is also present). For VAT-inclusive lines, Merlin back-calculates the ex-VAT base and derives the VAT amount by subtraction (gross − net) rather than multiplying the net by the tax rate. This avoids the cent-drift that occurs when rounding a back-calculated net before multiplying — for example, R700.00 inclusive at 15% would become R608.70 net, giving R608.70 × 15% = R91.31, totalling R700.01 instead of R700.00. Using subtraction instead gives exactly R700.00 − R608.70 = R91.30.
